title: Internal Energy
subtitle: Kinetic and potential energy of particles

steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: The internal energy of a substance is…
options: Only the kinetic energy of its particles
The total kinetic and potential energy stored by its particles
Only the potential energy stored between its particles
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Internal energy is the total energy stored by the particles — both their kinetic energy (due to motion) and their potential energy (due to the forces between them). Temperature is linked to average kinetic energy only, not internal energy.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! Internal energy = total kinetic energy + total potential energy of all the particles in the substance.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: AQA Physics specification 4.3.2 — Internal energy

steps.title: Internal Energy
steps.bodyHTML: Internal energy is the total energy stored by all the particles in a substance, equal to the sum of their kinetic energies (from movement) and their potential energies (from the forces between them). Heating a substance increases its internal energy — if no change of state occurs, the kinetic energy (and therefore temperature) increases; during a change of state, only the potential energy increases (temperature stays constant).

relationships: Internal energy = kinetic energy + potential energy of particles
Heating with no state change → KE increases → temperature rises
Heating during state change → PE increases → temperature constant
Temperature → linked to average KE of particles only

commonErrors: Thinking internal energy is the same as temperature
Forgetting that potential energy increases during a change of state
Confusing internal energy (total) with thermal energy or heat

aqa.questionText: Explain what happens to the internal energy and temperature of a substance when it is heated and its temperature rises (no change of state occurs).

steps.label: State what increases
steps.content: The internal energy of the substance increases as energy is transferred to it by heating.

steps.label: Link to kinetic energy
steps.content: Since there is no change of state, all the extra energy goes into increasing the kinetic energy of the particles (they move faster).
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — kinetic energy of particles increases

steps.label: Link to temperature
steps.content: Greater average kinetic energy of the particles means a higher temperature, so the temperature of the substance rises.
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — temperature rises as average KE increases

aqa.finalAnswer: Internal energy increases; kinetic energy of particles increases; temperature rises.

aqa.questionText: When ice melts at 0°C, what happens to the internal energy and temperature?

options.label: B
options.text: Internal energy increases; temperature stays constant

options.label: C
options.text: Internal energy stays constant; temperature stays constant

options.label: D
options.text: Internal energy decreases; temperature stays constant

markSchemeSteps: During melting, energy is absorbed to break bonds → potential energy increases → internal energy increases
Kinetic energy unchanged → temperature stays at 0°C ✓
aqa.commonMistake: Option A incorrectly links increased internal energy to a temperature rise — during a state change, all extra energy goes into potential energy, not kinetic energy.
